title: Mystery Explosion in Space: Could It Be a 'Superkilonova'?
summary:
When massive stars die, they explode as supernovae. When two neutron stars (the ultra-dense cores of dead stars) collide, they create a kilonova — a rare explosion that forges heavy elements like gold and uranium. Until now, scientists had only confirmed one kilonova: GW170817, detected in 2017.

But a new candidate, named AT2025ulz, is puzzling astronomers. It started like a kilonova — fading rapidly and glowing red, just like the 2017 event. But after a few days, it brightened again, shifted to bluer light, and showed hydrogen in its spectrum — classic signs of a supernova. Some researchers lost interest, thinking it was just an ordinary supernova unrelated to the earlier gravitational-wave signal.

Not Mansi Kasliwal's team at Caltech. "At first, for about three days, the eruption looked just like the first kilonova in 2017," says Kasliwal. "Everybody was intensely trying to observe and analyze it, but then it started to look more like a supernova, and some astronomers lost interest. Not us."

Kasliwal and her colleagues propose that AT2025ulz could be something entirely new: a superkilonova — a kilonova triggered by a supernova. The idea has been theorized but never observed. Here's how it might work: a massive star explodes as a supernova, but the explosion creates two small neutron stars that then spiral together and collide, producing a kilonova hidden inside the supernova debris.

The first clue came on August 18, 2025, when LIGO and Virgo detectors picked up a gravitational-wave signal. The signal suggested at least one of the colliding objects was unusually small — less massive than a typical neutron star. Hours later, the Zwicky Transient Facility spotted a fading red source about 1.3 billion light-years away, in the same region of the sky.

If confirmed, this would be a groundbreaking discovery. "We are continuing to analyze the data," says David Reitze, executive director of LIGO. "It's clear that at least one of the colliding objects is less massive than a typical neutron star."

Kasliwal's team published their findings in The Astrophysical Journal Letters. The mystery continues — and that's what makes science so exciting.
why_it_matters: This discovery could transform our understanding of stellar evolution and the origin of heavy elements. It also teaches the value of persistence in science – not giving up when data gets confusing.

keywords.explanation: Rare explosion from two neutron stars colliding
The first confirmed kilonova, detected in 2017
The new candidate that may be a superkilonova
Hypothetical explosion combining a supernova and a kilonova

questions.question: What did AT2025ulz show after a few days that made some scientists lose interest?
What is the proposed name for a kilonova triggered by a supernova?
What detected the gravitational-wave signal on August 18, 2025?
What did the gravitational-wave signal suggest about one of the colliding objects?
options: It was less massive than a typical neutron star
questions.correct_answer: It was less massive than a typical neutron star

background_read: The first confirmed kilonova, GW170817, was detected in 2017 by LIGO and Virgo, and it proved that neutron star mergers produce heavy elements like gold and uranium.
Kilonovas are extremely rare; only one had been observed until this new candidate.
LIGO (Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory) and Virgo are large detectors that measure ripples in spacetime caused by massive cosmic events.
The Zwicky Transient Facility (ZTF) is a powerful survey telescope that scans the sky for sudden changes, like explosions.
Article_Structure: LEAD: A new explosion, AT2025ulz, is baffling astronomers because it looks like both a kilonova and a supernova.
└─ SPECIFIC: 'At first it looked like the 2017 kilonova. Everybody was excited. Then it looked like a supernova, and some lost interest.'
KEY EVENT: On August 18, 2025, LIGO and Virgo detect a gravitational-wave signal suggesting an unusually small collision.
├─ WHO: Mansi Kasliwal's team at Caltech
├─ WHEN: Observations began after the gravitational-wave trigger
└─ WHERE: About 1.3 billion light-years away, seen by Zwicky Transient Facility
EVIDENCE: The explosion started red (kilonova) then turned blue and showed hydrogen (supernova).
PROPOSAL: Kasliwal's team suggests a 'superkilonova' – a kilonova inside a supernova.
└─ HOW: A supernova creates two neutron stars that then spiral together and collide.
OPEN QUESTION: Is this really a superkilonova? 'We are continuing to analyze the data' – David Reitze, LIGO.

perspectives.description: If confirmed, AT2025ulz would be the first observed superkilonova, opening a new category of cosmic events and explaining how heavy elements form in the universe.
Some astronomers dismissed the event as an ordinary supernova after seeing hydrogen, potentially missing a groundbreaking discovery. This shows the risk of jumping to conclusions.
The event fits a pattern: rare cosmic phenomena often challenge existing categories. The superkilonova hypothesis is plausible but needs more data from future observations.
Upcoming telescopes like the Vera Rubin Observatory will spot many more transient events, increasing the chances of finding similar hybrid explosions and settling the debate.
